text.title: microservices-design Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for microservices-design. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Each service is a completely independent deployment unit: its own repository (or isolated module), its own Dockerfile, its own database, its own CI/CD pipeline.
rule.rationale: Shared infrastructure creates hidden coupling. A database schema change in a shared DB can break all services that read it, defeating independent deployability.

rule.statement: Every service exposes a /health endpoint. The health check returns 200 only when the service and its critical dependencies (DB, broker) are reachable.
rule.rationale: Load balancers and orchestrators use health checks to route traffic. A service that responds 200 while its DB is down accepts requests it cannot fulfill.

rule.statement: Prefer asynchronous messaging (events) over synchronous HTTP for cross-service state changes. Use synchronous HTTP only for queries where the caller needs an immediate response.
rule.rationale: Synchronous chains create availability dependencies: if any service in the chain is down, the entire chain fails. Async events allow each service to process when available.

rule.statement: HTTP clients must set explicit timeouts. Default timeout is 10 seconds maximum — never use no timeout in production.
rule.rationale: Without timeouts, a slow downstream service holds a thread/connection indefinitely, cascading into resource exhaustion in the caller.

rule.statement: Every HTTP call to another service must go through a circuit breaker. When the failure threshold is exceeded, the breaker opens and fails fast instead of waiting for timeout.
rule.rationale: Without a circuit breaker, a slow or unavailable downstream service holds connections and threads in the caller, cascading into resource exhaustion across the entire system.

rule.statement: Circuit breaker states: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (fail fast, reset after recovery_timeout), HALF_OPEN (test with limited calls, close if successful).
rule.rationale: HALF_OPEN prevents the breaker from staying open indefinitely after the downstream service recovers. It allows gradual traffic restoration.

rule.statement: Every saga step must have a compensating action written before the happy path is merged. Compensation runs in reverse order when any step fails.
rule.rationale: A saga without compensations is an incomplete transaction. If step 2 fails after step 1 succeeds, step 1's side effect is permanent and cannot be undone.

rule.statement: Prefer orchestration (one central orchestrator) over choreography when more than 3-4 services participate. Choreography with many services creates spaghetti event flows that are impossible to debug.
rule.rationale: Orchestration centralizes the saga state machine and makes the flow readable as code. Choreography distributes it across services with no single place to observe the overall state.
